title: A Journey That Stayed with You
prompt: Describe a trip or journey that had a strong effect on you — it could be a holiday, a short trip, or even an unexpected journey. Tell the story of what happened, explain what made it memorable, and reflect on what it taught you about yourself or the world.

examples.title: My First Solo Trip
examples.body:
One journey that I remember very well was the first time I travelled alone. I was twenty-two years old and I went to Portugal for a week. Before the trip, I was quite nervous because I had never travelled without my family or friends.

I had a small problem at the beginning — my flight was delayed for four hours. At first, I felt very stressed and I didn't know what to do. But then I decided to find a café in the airport and have some food. I started talking to another traveller who was also waiting, and we had a very nice conversation. That helped me feel much calmer.

When I arrived in Lisbon, I was amazed by how beautiful the city was. I visited museums, tried local food, and walked around for hours every day. I got lost a few times, but I always managed to find my way. I also met some other travellers at my hostel and we went to a fado music show together one evening.

What I learned from this trip is that I am more capable than I thought. Before going, I was worried about everything — the language, the transport, what to do if something went wrong. But actually, I managed fine, and even the small problems were not so bad.

It also taught me that travelling alone gives you a lot of freedom. You can do exactly what you want, when you want. I would recommend it to anyone who hasn't tried it. Since that trip, I have travelled alone many more times and I always enjoy it.

examples.title: The Trip That Unsettled Me
examples.body:
There are journeys you take and largely forget, and then there are those that quietly rearrange something in you. A trip I took to rural Morocco about five years ago falls firmly into the second category.

I had been travelling for a couple of weeks and had spent most of my time in cities — Marrakech, Fes — which were extraordinary but also, in a sense, legible. There were tourist structures, things I could orient myself around. Then a friend I met on the road suggested we hire a car and drive into the High Atlas region without any particular plan. I'm not someone who usually travels that way, and I agreed partly to prove to myself that I could.

What followed was three days of improvised navigation through mountain villages where we were often the only outsiders. Communication was limited — we had no Arabic and very little French beyond the basics — and yet the hospitality we encountered was completely disarming. We were invited into a family's home for tea and stayed for dinner. The warmth was unconditional in a way I found almost difficult to process, because it came with no expectation of anything in return.

What unsettled me, and I mean this in a productive sense, was realising how much of my worldview had been shaped by a kind of low-grade, barely conscious wariness — towards strangers, towards unfamiliarity, towards situations I couldn't control. That trip stripped those reflexes back in a way that a more comfortable, planned journey simply couldn't have.

I came home with a genuine shift in how I thought about trust and generosity. I don't want to sentimentalise the experience or turn it into something neater than it was. There were moments of frustration and genuine discomfort. But that discomfort, I think, was exactly the point — it was the thing that made the experience instructive rather than just pleasant.

I still think about that family occasionally. I wonder whether they have any memory of us, or whether we were simply one brief, unremarkable visit among many. Either way, the encounter meant something to me that I haven't fully finished making sense of.
